skill.name: chrome-devtools-debugging
skill.description: Debug web applications through Chrome DevTools MCP, including console logs, network requests, performance traces, JavaScript evaluation, and authenticated session analysis.
skill.summary: Inspect Chrome console output, network traffic, performance data, and browser state through DevTools MCP.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, network, script, storage, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found no prompt injection, hidden malware, credential exfiltration endpoint, or confirmed malicious intent; most command and crypto alerts are Markdown example false positives. The skill still has medium risk because its intended DevTools workflows can read authenticated browser storage, cookies, request headers, response bodies, and execute JavaScript in a live browser context.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Browser State Can Be Exposed
medium_findings.description: The documentation intentionally shows how to read and print localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies, and auth tokens from an authenticated Chrome session. This is useful for debugging, but it can expose secrets to AI transcripts, terminal logs, and shared reports if users do not redact output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly read browser storage, cookies, and token keys, then print the values. The intent is debugging rather than theft, so the risk is data exposure instead of confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: JavaScript Evaluation Runs In Browser Context
medium_findings.description: The skill exposes DevTools JavaScript evaluation against the active page. If used on sensitive sites or with untrusted expressions, it can read page data, change application state, or interact with authenticated sessions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly describes and demonstrates evaluate_script usage. This is an intentional DevTools capability, but it has real impact when attached to authenticated pages.

medium_findings.title: Network Inspection May Reveal Sensitive Headers And Bodies
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to inspect network requests with headers, bodies, auth flows, and response details. This can expose Authorization headers, cookies, API payloads, or personal data during normal debugging.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly describe full request and response details and auth-related headers. I found no endpoint that sends this data elsewhere, so this remains an exposure risk, not malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The many external command alerts come from Markdown examples that run Python wrapper snippets with uv. They are visible user-invoked debugging commands, not hidden install hooks or automatic execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns appear inside fenced Markdown examples and match the skill purpose. No evidence found of covert shell execution outside user-facing documentation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Local Or Example Targets
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL and IP findings point to localhost remote debugging endpoints or example web addresses. They do not identify a suspicious remote collection server.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are 127.0.0.1 Chrome debugging addresses or generic example domains. No evidence found of a malicious or unexplained external destination.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Dynamic Import Alerts Are Scanner Artifacts
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and dynamic import alerts do not correspond to cryptographic operations or JavaScript dynamic imports in context. The cited lines are metadata, table headers, comments, or normal Python import examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited context found no MD5/SHA1-style code and no JavaScript import() expression. The alerts appear caused by token matching in documentation text.

dangerous_patterns.title: DevTools Access To Authenticated Sessions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill connects to an existing Chrome session and inspects authenticated state. Users should treat outputs as sensitive and avoid running it on accounts or pages outside their authorization.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly supports authenticated session debugging and provides examples for auth-related storage and requests. This is a legitimate but sensitive capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser JavaScript Evaluation
dangerous_patterns.description: The evaluate_script workflow executes JavaScript in the inspected page. This can access DOM data, browser storage, and application state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The capability is explicitly documented as JavaScript execution in browser context. The risk depends on what expressions users run and which page is attached.

dangerous_patterns.title: Request Header And Body Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: Network request inspection can reveal sensitive headers, cookies, request bodies, and response bodies. Users should redact secrets before sharing output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation states that full request and response details are available. That is normal for DevTools debugging but can expose confidential data.

content.user_title: Debug Chrome Apps with DevTools MCP
content.value_statement: Browser bugs are hard to diagnose when console logs, network failures, and auth state are hidden. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Chrome DevTools MCP workflows for focused web debugging.
actual_capabilities: Lists browser console messages and filters errors, warnings, and other message types.
Retrieves detailed console message context, including stack traces when available.
Lists network requests by resource type and identifies failed, slow, or API requests.
Retrieves full request and response details through Chrome DevTools MCP wrappers.
Runs JavaScript expressions in the current page for DOM, storage, and state inspection.
Starts and analyzes performance traces using the documented MCP wrapper modules.
limitations: Requires Chrome remote debugging or Chrome DevTools MCP to be installed and reachable.
Does not provide broad browser automation features such as screenshots or form filling.
May expose sensitive cookies, tokens, headers, and response bodies in debugging output.
Works only against browser pages and sessions that the user can access locally.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Console Failures
use_cases.description: Inspect browser errors, warnings, stack traces, and application state after a broken user flow.

use_cases.title: Investigate API Problems
use_cases.description: Correlate failed network requests with console output, headers, response bodies, and slow endpoints.

use_cases.title: Review Authenticated Sessions
use_cases.description: Debug login flows and authenticated pages by checking storage, cookies, requests, and visible app state.

prompt_templates.title: Check Console Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chrome-devtools-debugging skill to inspect the current Chrome tab for console errors and warnings. Summarize the likely cause and next debugging step.

prompt_templates.title: Find Failed Requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Chrome DevTools MCP to list recent XHR and fetch requests. Identify failed or slow requests and explain how they relate to console errors.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Auth Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to inspect my authenticated browser session after login. Check storage and auth-related network requests, but redact tokens and cookies in the response.

prompt_templates.title: Correlate Performance And Network
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Chrome DevTools MCP to reload the page, inspect slow network requests, review console errors, and analyze available performance trace insights.

output_examples.input: Inspect the current page for console problems.
output: Found two error messages and one warning in the active tab.
The main failure appears after an API request returns a server error.
Recommended next step: inspect that request and compare the response with the client error handler.

output_examples.input: Find why the dashboard is not loading data.
output: The dashboard request completed with an authorization failure.
The console also shows a data parsing error after the failed response.
Auth values were present but should be redacted before sharing logs.
Several document and script requests loaded normally, but one API request exceeded the chosen threshold.
The slow request is the best first target for backend timing and caching review.
A performance trace can confirm whether rendering or network time dominates.

best_practices: Run Chrome remote debugging only on trusted local machines and close it after debugging.
Redact cookies, tokens, Authorization headers, and personal data before sharing outputs.
Start with console and failed request summaries before retrieving full request bodies.
anti_patterns: Do not attach DevTools to accounts or sessions that you are not authorized to inspect.
Do not paste untrusted JavaScript expressions into evaluate_script workflows.
Do not publish raw DevTools output that includes secrets, cookies, or user data.

faq.question: What does this skill help debug?
faq.answer: It helps inspect console messages, network requests, performance traces, page state, and authenticated browser sessions through Chrome DevTools MCP.

faq.question: Does it take screenshots or fill forms?
faq.answer: No. The skill documentation says to use a browser automation or discovery skill for screenshots, form filling, and basic navigation tasks.

faq.question: What setup is required?
faq.answer: Chrome must expose DevTools access through a remote debugging port, auto-connect support, or a compatible Chrome DevTools MCP server.

faq.question: Can it inspect logged-in pages?
faq.answer: Yes. It can inspect authenticated Chrome sessions, which means output may include sensitive storage, cookies, headers, and response data.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning. The reviewed files show legitimate debugging guidance, but users must handle sensitive browser data carefully.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Use it only on authorized sessions, limit output collection, redact secrets, and avoid running untrusted JavaScript in the browser context.
